Name: Source Note
JiaHui Zhang: The passages are adapted from Nathaniel Hawthorne, The Blithedale Romance. Originally published in 1852.
Five-and-twenty years ago, at the epoch of this story, there dwelt, a man whom we shall call Fauntleroy.
column3: at twenty five years ago, at the begining of the epoch, a man whom we call Fuenleory.

JiaHui Zhang: A man of wealth, and magnificent tastes, and prodigal expenditure. His home might almost be styled a palace, his habits in the ordinary sense, princely.
column3: A man with wealth, a magnificiant taste, his home are almost styled a palace, his habits in ordinary sense

JiaHui Zhang: His whole being seemed to have crystallized itself into an external splendor, wherewith he glittered in the eyes of the world.
column3: his whole being seem to be crystallized itself into an external, the eyes of the world

JiaHui Zhang: He saw himself in imminent peril of losing all that had heretofore distinguished him; and, conscious of no innate worth to fall back upon, he recoiled from this calamity, with the instinct of a soul shrinking from annihilation.
column3: he saw himself in imminent, with the instinct of a soul shrinking from annihilation

JiaHui Zhang: Fauntleroy's guilt was discovered. He fled; his wife perished by the necessity of her innate nobleness, in its alliance with a being so ignoble; and betwixt her mother's death and her father's ignominy, his daughter was left worse than orphaned.
